transcript:
Interviewer: Thank you for taking the time to speak with me today, Mr. Sharma. I'd like to understand how Priya has been performing since she joined your team three months ago.

Rajesh Sharma: Of course. Well, let me start by saying that Priya has been a pleasant surprise. When you first told me you were sending a young consultant, I was skeptical. But she's proved me wrong.

Interviewer: Can you give me some specific examples?

Rajesh Sharma: Yes. The biggest thing she's done is with our production scheduling. Before she came, we were doing everything on whiteboards and WhatsApp messages. Very chaotic. She created this Excel-based tracking system—nothing fancy, but it works. Now I know exactly which machine is doing what, and my supervisors update it daily.

Interviewer: How has the team responded to this?

Rajesh Sharma: Mixed, honestly. The younger supervisors adapted quickly—they actually like having a system. But my old shift supervisor, Mr. Desai, he's been here 20 years, he initially resisted. Said this was unnecessary paperwork. But Priya didn't push him. She spent time understanding his workflow, then showed him how the tracker would reduce his Sunday reporting time from 3 hours to 30 minutes. After that, he was on board.

Interviewer: That's great. What about her reliability?

Rajesh Sharma: Very reliable. She's here by 8:30 AM every day, often stays late. If she says she'll deliver something by Friday, it's done by Thursday evening. I've never had to follow up with her.

Interviewer: Any areas where you think she could improve?

Rajesh Sharma: One thing—sometimes she's too diplomatic. There was an incident where a vendor delivered substandard raw material. She documented it perfectly, but she didn't escalate it strongly enough. I had to step in and have a tough conversation with the vendor. She needs to learn that sometimes you have to be firm.

Interviewer: Has her work had any impact on your business metrics?

Rajesh Sharma: Definitely. Our on-time delivery has improved—we were at maybe 65-70% before, now we're consistently above 80%. And I'm spending less time firefighting, so I can focus on strategic things. The tracking system she built, I think we'll keep using it even after she leaves.

Interviewer: Overall, how would you rate her performance?

Rajesh Sharma: I'd give her a 7 out of 10. She's good, better than I expected. She's built something useful. But she needs more experience handling difficult situations before I'd call her exceptional. But she's learning, and she listens to feedback.

Interviewer: Is there anything else you'd like to add?

Rajesh Sharma: Just that I hope you send more people like her. And I'd like her to stay for the full 6 months if possible. We have some other areas—inventory management, quality control documentation—where I think she could help us.

Interviewer: Good afternoon, Ms. Deshmukh. Thank you for making time. I'd like to discuss Vikram's progress. He's been with GreenPak for two months now.

Anita Deshmukh: Good afternoon. Well, I'll be direct with you—it's not going as well as I'd hoped.

Interviewer: I appreciate your honesty. Can you elaborate?

Anita Deshmukh: The idea was that Vikram would help us streamline our order processing. We're a growing company, and our manual system is breaking down. But two months in, I don't see any changes.

Interviewer: What has he been working on?

Anita Deshmukh: That's part of the problem—I'm not entirely sure. He's been doing something with data analysis. He showed me some charts about order patterns. Interesting, but not what we need right now. We needed someone to fix the process, not analyze it to death.

Interviewer: Has he discussed his approach with you?

Anita Deshmukh: He had one presentation three weeks in. I told him then that analysis is fine, but we need action. Since then, I've been too busy to follow up closely, and he hasn't proactively updated me.

Interviewer: What about his day-to-day presence?

Anita Deshmukh: He's punctual, polite, gets along with everyone. No complaints there. The team likes him. But liking someone and getting results are different things.

Interviewer: Has there been anything positive?

Anita Deshmukh: He did help our dispatch team organize their documentation. That was useful. And the order pattern analysis, while not what I wanted, did show that we have a spike every Wednesday—which we're now preparing for. So it wasn't useless. Just... not the priority.

Interviewer: What would you need to see to feel better about the engagement?

Anita Deshmukh: I need a concrete plan with deadlines. I need weekly updates without me having to ask. And I need to see at least one process actually changed—not just analyzed—in the next month. Otherwise, I'm not sure it makes sense to continue.

Interviewer: On a scale of 1 to 10, where would you place him right now?

Anita Deshmukh: Right now? Maybe a 4. He's not harmful, but he's not delivering what we need. But I haven't given up yet. I think with clearer direction, he might still turn it around.

Interviewer: Any final thoughts?

Anita Deshmukh: I think there's a gap between what we expected and what was communicated to him. Maybe we need to reset expectations together—you, me, and Vikram. If we're clear about priorities, I'm willing to give him another month.

Interviewer: Mr. Kumar, Anjali has been with Bharat Textiles for five months now. How would you describe her contribution?

Suresh Kumar: Transformational. I don't use that word lightly. But Anjali has genuinely changed how we operate.

Interviewer: That's strong praise. What specifically has she done?

Suresh Kumar: When she arrived, our loom utilization was around 55%. We had machines sitting idle because of poor planning, material delays, and no visibility into maintenance schedules. She built a complete production planning system—not just a spreadsheet, but a proper system with daily targets, material requirement calculations, and preventive maintenance scheduling.

Interviewer: And what was the impact?

Suresh Kumar: Loom utilization is now 78%. That's a 23 percentage point improvement. Our monthly output has increased by 35% without adding any new machines or people. The maintenance team now has a clear schedule—breakdowns have reduced significantly because we're doing preventive maintenance, not reactive repairs.

Interviewer: How did the team adapt to these changes?

Suresh Kumar: That's where she really excelled. She didn't just design the system and hand it over. She trained every shift supervisor personally. She spent nights on the shop floor during the transition. When the system had issues in the first week, she was there troubleshooting. The supervisors—who were initially resistant—now say they can't imagine going back to the old way.

Interviewer: What about the workers on the floor?

Suresh Kumar: She learned Tamil. My workers mostly speak Tamil, and Anjali is from Kerala. But she learned basic Tamil in three months so she could communicate directly with them. That won her enormous respect. They don't see her as an outsider anymore—she's part of the team.

Interviewer: Has she built any systems that will outlast her presence?

Suresh Kumar: Yes. The production planning system is fully documented—in English and Tamil. She's trained a junior engineer to manage it. The daily review meetings she established—we still do them even when she's not here. She's built processes, not dependencies.

Interviewer: Any areas for improvement?

Suresh Kumar: Minor things. She sometimes takes on too much personally rather than delegating. I've told her she can't be everywhere. But this comes from her commitment, not from inability to delegate. She's learning to step back.

Interviewer: What score would you give her?

Suresh Kumar: 9 out of 10. I don't give 10 because there's always room to grow. But she's the best consultant we've worked with. I've already spoken to your team about extending her engagement—we have other areas where we need this kind of transformation.

Interviewer: Any other KPIs she's impacted?

Suresh Kumar: Several. On-time delivery has gone from 72% to 91%. Our quality rejection rate has dropped from 8% to 3% because the preventive maintenance means machines are calibrated properly. Even employee attendance has improved—workers feel more motivated when they see systems working. Our customer complaints have reduced by 60%.

Interviewer: That's remarkable. Final thoughts?

Suresh Kumar: I was skeptical about this program. A young consultant in a textile mill? I thought it was a mismatch. But Anjali proved that good systems thinking works in any industry. She respected our experience while bringing fresh perspective. That balance is rare.
